fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to add a new feature to the repo service around snapshot build cleanup. Basically, after someone pushes a new snapshot build, the old build artifacts should get cleaned up automatically so we don't accumulate junk. The user-facing complaint is that storage keeps ballooning after repeated snapshot deployments and browsing those version folders gets super messy with hundreds of stale files.

The cleanup should leave behind only the metadata-related files for the snapshot version — those checksum sidecars we use everywhere need to stay (same pattern we followed for the mirror and deploy flows). Old build timestamp entries should be removed but the core metadata file and all its integrity companion files must survive.

A few things I'm not 100% sure about: how many checksum formats we actually support (I think it's the same set we standardized on for the descriptor generation work?), and whether the new timestamp needs to be tracked or just used as a trigger. Also someone mentioned something about the base version naming convention with that dash-R pattern — not sure if that affects the path structure or just the filename matching.

Path structure should follow the same conventions as the rest of the artifact storage stuff. Please check how the existing deploy and metadata modules handle version paths to stay consistent. We need this scoped to the specific version snapshot folder, not the whole artifact tree.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came back: after pruning, the snapshot version path should end up with exactly five files left and only those metadata files stay around: maven-metadata.xml, maven-metadata.xml.md5, maven-metadata.xml.sha1, maven-metadata.xml.sha256, and maven-metadata.xml.sha512. So yes, the supported checksum sidecar extensions here are exactly .md5, .sha1, .sha256, and .sha512, and every metadata file keeps all four companions, same as what we already do in descriptor generation and the artifact deploy/delete modules.

Also confirming the version naming piece: the base version can look like '1.0.0-R0.1', with that dash-R infix as part of the release qualifier, and the snapshot folder is just that plus '-SNAPSHOT', so for example '1.0.0-R0.1-SNAPSHOT' is the version path segment. That naming convention does not change the directory structure at all, it just affects the version/folder name we match within.

On the build identifiers, the old entries we want removed are the timestamped ones in the '<timestamp>-<buildNumber>' shape, like '20220101.213700-1' and '20220101.213701-2'. The new timestamp, for example '20220101.213702', is there as the trigger/reference for which snapshot was just pushed, but we are not trying to remove the new build's own artifact files as part of that.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: pruneSnapshotBuilds(artifactId: String, versionPath: String, baseVersion: String, oldBuilds: List<String>, newTimestamp: String): List<String>

trigger_keywords: what path format does the output use
how are remaining file paths reported
does the output include the full version path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each remaining file is reported as a full relative path including the version_path prefix, e.g. 'group/artifact/1.0.0-R0.1-SNAPSHOT/maven-metadata.xml'. The output lists one path per line using the 'remaining=<path>' key format, with five lines total.

trigger_keywords: does the new timestamp get stored
is the new build timestamp recorded anywhere
what happens to the new_timestamp parameter after pruning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The new timestamp is used only as a trigger to initiate the pruning process. It is not itself stored as a new artifact entry in the output of the prune operation. The prune result only reflects what metadata files remain.

trigger_keywords: does artifact_id affect the path
how is artifact_id used in pruning
what role does the artifact identifier play in cleanup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The artifact_id (e.g. 'artifact') is used for filename matching to identify which files in the version folder belong to old build timestamps of that artifact. It does not change the version_path directory structure itself.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'storeArtifactFile', 'removeArtifactFile', 'requestMirroredArtifact', 'requestArtifactVersions', 'createDescriptor', 'applyDownloadFilter', 'pruneSnapshotBuilds', 'orderVersionLabels', 'joinRepositoryPath', 'validateRepositoryPath', 'orderDirectoryEntries', 'parseNetworkProxy', 'replacePlaceholders', 'createConfiguration', 'manageAccessToken', 'authenticateCredentials', 'listVisibleRepositories', 'requestArtifactEntry', 'requestDirectoryListing'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same pattern we followed for the mirror and deploy flows
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The file path convention used in feature10_1_artifact_deploy.json and feature11_mirror_lookup.json where artifact paths are stored and reported as relative paths under the repository/version directory
feature12_2_descriptor_generation.json — specifically the metadata_has_previous, metadata_has_new, and checksum-related fields that establish the four-format checksum standard

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing deploy and metadata modules in the codebase for the conventions used, particularly how snapshot version paths and checksum sidecar files are handled in the descriptor generation and artifact storage features.

feature10_1_artifact_deploy.json.description: Stores an uploaded artifact file under the requested repository path and makes the stored entry discoverable.

feature10_2_artifact_delete.json.description: Requires matching write authorization to remove a stored artifact file.

feature11_mirror_lookup.json.description: Fetches allowed artifacts from configured remote repositories and rejects mirrored paths outside the allow rules.

feature12_1_metadata_lookup.json.description: Reads artifact metadata and returns either the latest matching version or all versions matching a prefix.

feature12_2_descriptor_generation.json.description: Generates a descriptor document for a new version and updates artifact metadata to advertise the new version.

cases.expected_output:
generated=true
pom_group=true
pom_artifact=true
pom_version=true
metadata_release=true
metadata_latest=true
metadata_has_previous=true
metadata_has_new=true
metadata_updated_prefix=true

feature13_download_filtering.json.description: Allows a registered file-resolution filter to transform downloaded content and update the reported content length.

feature14_snapshot_pruning.json.description: After a new snapshot deployment, removes deprecated snapshot build artifacts and leaves only metadata checksum files.

feature1_version_ordering.json.description: Orders mixed version labels from lowest to highest using repository listing rules.

feature2_1_path_joining.json.description: Joins two repository path fragments while normalizing repeated separators and preserving non-ASCII names.

feature2_2_path_validation.json.description: Rejects repository paths that would escape the repository root or encode an absolute drive path.

feature3_directory_entry_ordering.json.description: Orders directory entries before files and applies version-aware ordering within each group.

cases.expected_output:
entry=directory:AlphaTool
entry=directory:Zeolite
entry=directory:1.0.1
entry=directory:1.0.2
entry=file:Apposite
entry=file:Lolita
entry=file:1.0.2
entry=file:1.0.3

feature4_proxy_parsing.json.description: Parses textual proxy definitions into network proxy type, host, and port fields.

feature5_2_placeholder_symbol_validation.description: Reports a normalized error when a placeholder token contains a multi-byte symbol.

feature5_placeholder_replacement.json.description: Streams byte content and replaces configured placeholder tokens with their configured values.

feature6_configuration_generation.json.description: Generates default configuration files for the requested configuration scope in an empty working directory.

feature7_access_token_lifecycle.json.description: Creates, updates, deletes, finds, and regenerates access tokens while exposing stable token state.

feature8_credential_authentication.json.description: Authenticates credentials against stored access-token secrets and reports either token identity or an HTTP authentication error.

feature9_1_repository_visibility_listing.description: Lists repositories visible to an anonymous or repository-authorized client.

feature9_2_artifact_entry_access.json.description: Returns file details from public or authorized repositories and returns a normalized HTTP error for unauthorized private access.

feature9_3_directory_index_access.json.description: Rejects anonymous directory listing for hidden or private repositories with an HTTP error signal.

cases.expected_output:
status=error
http_status=401
message=You need to provide credentials.
